rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order Establishing Defendant Mahmoud Razzaghis Admissions
rulings.Rulings: The matter is on calendar for Thursday, May 3, 2018, Line 2, Plaintiff Mohammed Elahi's Motion For Order Establishing Defendant Mahmoud Razzaghis Admissions. Pro Tem Judge Sanford Kingsley, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: No written opposition has been filed to the motion. The motion establishes that Plaintiff's Second Set of Requests for Admission were served by mail on Defendant on February 21, 2018 and no responses have been served. The motion is granted and pursuant to CCP section 2033.280 (b), the genuineness of any documents and the truth of any matters specified in Plaintiff's Second Set of Requests for Admission are deemed admitted. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to skmediate@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: DEPARTMENT OF FAIR EMPLOYMENT AND HOUSING AN VS. FOREVER 21 RETAIL INC A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, May 3, 2018, Line 6, DEFENDANT FOREVER 21 RETAIL, INC. MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION. (Begining of Part 1 of 2) Defendant Forever 21 Retail, Inc.'s ("Forever 21") motion for summary adjudication is DENIED. The court denies summary adjudication on the claim for punitive damages. Forever 21 correctly notes that the complaint does not expressly seek punitive damages. There is authority holding that punitive damages can be allowed even where the complaint lacks such a prayer if the body of the complaint alleges conduct that will support the claim, however. (See Forte v. Nolfi (1972) 25 Cal.App.3d 656, 687.) Here, the allegations of discrimination and retaliation support the claim. In addition, DFEH calendared a motion to amend the complaint that is currently set for May 11, 2018, to cure any alleged pleading defect and Forever 21 addresses the merits of the claim. There is no prejudice to Forever 21 based upon the purported pleading error. The Department of Fair Employment and Housing ("DFEH") can seek punitive damages in the employment context and Forever 21 overstates the holding of Dyna-Med, Inc. v. Fair Emp. & Housing Comm. (1987) 43 Cal.3d 1379.) In that case, the Supreme Court was limiting its review specifically to the issue of what types of damages the Fair Employment Housing Commission (i.e., the agency) could impose. The Legislature amended FEHA and disbanded the Commission. Since the court-not an agency-is adjudicating the punitives issue, the DFEH can seek recovery of that remedy. Gov't Code 12965(a) allows the DFEH to bring a claim on behalf of real parties. Gov't Code 12965(c) states that "[a] court may grant as relief in any action filed pursuant to subdivision (a) any relief a court is empowered to grant in a civil action brought pursuant to subdivision (b), in addition to any other relief that, in the judgment of the court, will effectuate the purpose of this part." (See also Commodore Home Systems v. Superior Court (1982) 32 Cal.3d 211, 215 (all forms of relief granted to civil litigants generally (including punitive damages where appropriate) may be awarded by court in civil action).) There is a triable issue of material fact whether DFEH can show oppression, fraud, or malice by clear and convincing evidence and that an officer, director, or managing agent of Forever 21 authorized, ratified, or committed the allegedly malicious conduct. (See Cruz v. HomeBase (2000) 83 Cal.App.4th 160, 167; White v. Ultramar (2009) 21 Cal.4th 563, 573.) Forever 21's Senior Human Resources Manager (Chang) declares that the cited employees cannot determine corporate policy and Forever 21 does not have a language policy. In opposition, DFEH demonstrates a disputed issue of fact regarding the existence of an English-only policy. (End of part 1 of 2) =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: DEPARTMENT OF FAIR EMPLOYMENT AND HOUSING AN VS. FOREVER 21 RETAIL INC A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Forever 21 Retail, Inc. Motion For Summary Adjudication. (For Tentative Ruling Purposes Only Part 2 Of 2)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, May 3, 2018, Line 6, Defendant Forever 21 Retail, Inc. Motion For Summary Adjudication. (For Tentative Ruling Purposes Only Begining of Part 2 Of 2) Store Manager Ramos stated that there was an English-only policy (Leon Depo., 143:3-19.) There is also a dispute of fact whether a managing agent ratified such a policy. Assistant Manager Harris had a conversation with Regional Director Calderon about the policy, but Harris did not view it as discipline. (Harris Depo., 197:23-198:8.) Calderon admitted that the English-only policy had been ongoing for months (Calderon Depo., 146:15-147:14; 151:22-24.) Calderon ignored real party Leon when he complained about the policy and told him to go to HR. (Leon Depo., 177:3-19; 181:17-20.) Calderon did not take corrective action. (Calderon Depo., 136:16-137:5.) There is a dispute whether Calderon had enough authority to be considered a managing agent and whether he ratified the policy. (Calderon Depo., 16:3-17:17:7; 31:18-33:7; 220:4-221:22.) People with authority equal to or less than Calderon are routinely held to be managing agents for punitive damages purposes. (See White v. Ultramar (1999) 21 Cal.4th 563, 580 (zone manager); Davis v. Kiewit Pacific Co. (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 358, 373 (equal opportunities officer); Powerhouse Motorsports Ground, Inc. v. Yamaha Motor Corp. (2013) 221 Cal.App.4th 867, 886 (regional sales manager).) There is a triable issue of material fact regarding whether DFEH can establish the first cause of action for violation of Gov't Code 12951. As noted above, the facts belie Forever 21's assertion that there was no English-only policy. Numerous witnesses testified about the policy. Forever 21's argument that the policy needed to come from a director, officer or managing agent to establish liability under Gov't Code 12951 is erroneous. Forever 21 is conflating the standard for punitive damages with the standard for liability under the statute. To establish the statutory claim, DFEH merely needs to show that a supervisor took illegal action. "[E]mployers will be held liable if their supervisory employees take actions later found discriminatory." (Reno v. Baird (1998) 18 Cal.4th 640, 647 (italics deleted).) "A supervisor is the employer's agent for purposes of vicarious liability for unlawful discrimination." (Cal. Fair Employment and Housing Com'n v. Gemini Aluminum Corp. (2004) 122 Cal.App.4th 1004, 1015.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4 pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for DFEH is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. (End of Part 2 of 2) =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: MARTHA GILMORE ET AL VS. UBER TECHNOLOGIES, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Service For Lack Of Proper Service; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Thereof
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, May 3, 2018, Line 8, DEFENDANT OMAR DAHMASH'S Motion To Quash Service For Lack Of Proper Service. Defendant Omar Dahmash's motion to quash service is DENIED. Registered process server Feely declares under penalty of perjury that he personally served Dahmash in Belmont, CA on December 17, 2017. Evidence Code 647 provides: "The return of a process server registered pursuant to¿the Business and Professions Code¿establishes a presumption¿of the facts stated in the return." Dahmash fails to rebut the presumption; he merely asserts that he resided in San Diego on December 17, not that he was not served or that a different person was served. (See American Exp. Centurian Bank v. Zara (2011) 199 Cal.App.4th 383 (granting motion to quash where defendant declared he was not served, and description in process servers' declaration of person served did not match defendant). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4 pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Gilmore is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: BURNESS C. BROUSSARD VS. APPRENDA, INC. ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, May 3, 2018, Line 9, DEFENDANT APPRENDA, INC.'S DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendant Apprenda, Inc's demurrer to the first amended complaint's cause of action for tortious interference with prospective economic relations is OVERRULED. Liberally construed, the FAC pleads that plaintiff Broussard had a reasonably probable expectation of economic benefit from his relationships with Kismatic, Inc. and defendant Reilly. This benefit would have been realized through Broussard's receiving a fair share of any deal involving sale of Kismatic assets or stock, and, had it not been for Apprenda's interference, Broussard would not have lost the prospective benefit. California law permits pleading of factually inconsistent theories. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4 pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Mr. Broussard is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/RBU)
